summary: This notebook provides a beginner-friendly tutorial for image forgery detection using semantic segmentation. It demonstrates how to adapt a Mask R-CNN architecture for detecting tampered regions by treating forged areas as object instances. The author walks through EDA to handle highly variable image sizes, implements a custom dataset class to convert segmentation masks into bounding boxes and binary masks, and trains a lightweight model with MobileNetV3-Small. Key takeaways include handling multi-channel masks, resizing inputs to a fixed dimension, and converting model outputs into the competition's required RLE format.
competition_flow: The notebook performs EDA on image forgery detection data, trains a lightweight Mask R-CNN model to segment tampered regions, and generates a submission file with RLE-encoded masks for test images.
data_reading: Loads images using PIL.Image.open and masks using np.load from Kaggle input paths.
Analyzes unique image dimensions and mask file formats across train/test splits.
data_processing: Resizes all images and masks to a fixed 256x256 resolution.
Uses horizontal/vertical flips and random 90-degree rotations (p=0.5) for training augmentations.
Converts multi-channel masks to binary via thresholding and np.any.
Converts binary masks to bounding boxes using OpenCV contours for Mask R-CNN training.
Resizes predictions back to original test image dimensions and RLE encodes them for submission.
loss_functions: Mask R-CNN internal composite loss (sum of classification, box regression, and mask IoU losses)
insights: Variable image sizes are handled by resizing all inputs to a fixed 256x256 resolution.
Mask R-CNN can be repurposed for forgery detection by framing tampered regions as detectable object instances.
A lightweight backbone like MobileNetV3-Small enables feasible training on limited GPU resources.
RLE encoding is required for the competition submission format and is implemented via Fortran-order flattening.
